title: Darling getattrlist missing ATTR_CMN_NAME -> Ruby Dir.glob returns empty (breaks Homebrew empty_installation? + all glob)
description:
Cross-process directory-entry coherence bug surfaced as the final blocker for Homebrew source builds (dar-pk4).

REPRO (via brew, but it's a pure VFS issue):
- A long-lived process (brew/ruby) creates an empty dir D (formula.prefix / keg) and stats it.
- A forked+exec'd child (make install) writes files into D/{bin,lib,include,...}.
- The child exits; the parent re-reads D (Pathname.glob('D/*')) and sees it STILL EMPTY.
- A FRESH process (find/ls run after) AND the host filesystem both see all the files correctly.

EVIDENCE: with Homebrew's failure-rollback disabled (HOMEBREW_KEEP_FAILED_KEG), the lz4 keg on disk is fully populated (bin/lz4 238KB, lib/liblz4.1.10.0.dylib 153KB, headers, man, pkgconfig) and visible to a fresh process + host — yet brew's parent raised 'Empty installation'. A simpler fs-probe showed that child/exec'd/nested writes to /usr/local/Cellar DO persist and are visible to the SAME process tree's later ls; the bug specifically affects a process that cached/stat'd the directory as empty BEFORE another process populated it (stale per-process dir cache, no invalidation on external modification).

IMPACT: blocks every Homebrew --build-from-source install at the post-make-install emptiness check. Worked around in the test prefix with HOMEBREW_FRESH_KEG_CHECK=1 (re-check keg via fresh subprocess) to get a green install, but the proper fix is VFS-level dir-cache invalidation/coherence in darlingserver.

NEXT: locate where Darling caches directory contents per-process (vfs/dcache in darlingserver or libsystem) and invalidate on external writes / disable stale caching for bind-mounted prefix paths.
notes: Surfaced while proving dar-pk4 Option B (from-source brew install). Final blocker after SDKSettings.json + oso_prefix gate + NO_SANDBOX fixes. Belongs under epic dar-gwn (Make Homebrew usable under Darling).
close_reason: Fixed: implemented getattrlist ATTR_CMN_NAME + ATTR_CMN_OBJTYPE in libsystem_kernel (xnu d157c3f, darling 7df268d2d). Ruby Dir.glob + Homebrew empty_installation? now correct; full from-source brew install green with no workaround.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
REPRO NUANCE — minimal C repro does NOT reproduce. A single process that does opendir/readdir on an empty dir, then a child (system()) writes a file + subdir, then the SAME process opendir/readdir again → SEES the 2 new entries correctly (both /tmp and /usr/local/Cellar). parent stat nlink also updates 2->3. So the bug is NARROWER than 'a process that cached a dir can't see another process's writes' — plain opendir/readdir is coherent.

So the trigger is something more specific in the Homebrew/Ruby path. Candidates to test next:
- Ruby Dir.glob/Pathname.glob (may use a different syscall path / fts / caching) vs raw readdir.
- The keg dir is created by parent via mkpath then the BUILD child is a deep fork+exec chain (sh -> make -> install); maybe depth or an exec boundary matters.
- A retained open DIR*/fd or fts handle from an earlier glob in the long-lived ruby process.
- darlingserver microthread/vchroot state specific to the long-lived process.
Repro harness: /tmp/vfs-repro.sh (C). Next: write a Ruby repro using Dir.glob/Pathname.glob mirroring formula_installer empty_installation? exactly, and an fts-based C repro.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
ROOT CAUSE FOUND — dar-e1j is MISDIAGNOSED. This is NOT a VFS cross-process coherence bug. It is a missing getattrlist attribute in Darling's libsystem_kernel emulation.

CHAIN OF EVIDENCE (all via darling-debug-runner against darling-prefix-homebrew-test):
1. Multi-scenario C readdir test (pre-existing/negative-lookup/keg-subtree/retained-DIR*): ALL coherent. readdir/getdirentries works perfectly cross-process. So no coherence bug.
2. Ruby repro of empty_installation?: Dir.entries(keg)=full (readdir OK) but Pathname.glob/Dir.glob(keg/*)=[] EMPTY. Same process, same instant. => Dir.glob is broken, not coherence.
3. Dir.glob returns 0 for EVERY dir (/usr/bin=534 entries, /bin=47) regardless of freshness. Confirms general breakage.
4. Both portable-ruby 4.0.5 AND system ruby 2.6.3 fail identically. Not version-specific.
5. Pattern: Dir.glob("/*")=14 OK; Dir.glob("*",base:"/usr/bin")=534 OK; but Dir.glob("/usr/*")=0, Dir.glob("/bin/bash") literal=0 while File.exist?=true. => fails on every LITERAL path component, works when only "*" / direct base.
6. Ruby's dir.c replace_real_basename() calls getattrlist(path, ATTR_CMN_NAME, FSOPT_NOFOLLOW) on each literal component (Darwin case-preservation path, taken because Darling reports volume case-insensitive). On failure it treats the component as not-found -> glob returns [].
7. C test: getattrlist(path, ATTR_CMN_NAME) => EINVAL for every path. getattrlistbulk => ENOTSUP. fdopendir/openat/fstatat/chained-relative-openat ALL work.

EXACT DEFECT: src/external/xnu/darling/src/libsystem_kernel/emulation/include/xnu_syscall/bsd/helper/xattr/getattrlist_generic.c
  line 18: #define COMMON_SUPPORTED (ATTR_CMN_FNDRINFO | ATTR_CMN_OBJTAG)   <-- no ATTR_CMN_NAME
  line 77-78: if ((commonattr & COMMON_SUPPORTED) != commonattr) return -EINVAL;  <-- rejects ATTR_CMN_NAME
Template is used by sys_getattrlist / sys_getattrlistat (HAS_PATH=1) and sys_fgetattrlist (HAS_PATH=0). Userland shim, not kernel/duct-tape.

IMPACT: GLOBAL, far beyond Homebrew. Any macOS program using Dir.glob / glob(3)-with-realcase / getattrlistbulk / NSDirectoryEnumerator is affected. The Homebrew "Empty installation" was just one symptom; the HOMEBREW_FRESH_KEG_CHECK workaround masked it. Fixing ATTR_CMN_NAME removes the need for that hack.

FIX: implement ATTR_CMN_NAME in getattrlist_generic.c — pack an attrreference_t + the basename string (basename of the supplied path for HAS_PATH; readlink /proc/self/fd/N for the fd variant). Also fixes the precondition for un-stubbing getattrlistbulk (which additionally needs ATTR_CMN_RETURNED_ATTRS). Volume case-sensitivity (ATTR_VOL_CAPABILITIES, _PC_CASE_SENSITIVE=11) is also unsupported (returns EINVAL) — secondary.

Repro scripts: /tmp/run-vfsr2.sh, /tmp/run-rbvfs.sh, /tmp/run-galb.sh (getattrlistbulk ENOTSUP), /tmp/run-fdo.sh, /tmp/run-at.sh, /tmp/run-gal.sh (getattrlist ATTR_CMN_NAME EINVAL).

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
RESOLVED + FIXED + VALIDATED.

Final root cause (supersedes the earlier "VFS coherence" and "ATTR_CMN_NAME-only" theories):
Darling's getattrlist emulation (libsystem_kernel getattrlist_generic.c) supported only
ATTR_CMN_FNDRINFO|ATTR_CMN_OBJTAG and returned EINVAL for any request containing another
attribute. The Darwin build of Ruby's dir.c globber (USE_NAME_ON_FS_REAL_BASENAME, always on
for macOS) resolves every LITERAL path component through replace_real_basename(), which calls
  getattrlist(path, {ATTR_CMN_NAME | ATTR_CMN_OBJTYPE}, ..., FSOPT_NOFOLLOW)
and on failure marks the component path_noent with NO lstat fallback. So EINVAL made
Dir.glob/Pathname.glob return [] for any pattern with a literal component (/usr/*, /bin/bash,
glob("bin") in cwd), while pure-"*" or base:-relative globs worked. Keg#empty_installation?
uses Pathname.glob, so a freshly-built keg always looked empty -> "Empty installation" abort.

Why the earlier fix attempt (ATTR_CMN_NAME only) didn't work: Ruby requests NAME *and* OBJTYPE
in the same attrlist; the combined request still failed the COMMON_SUPPORTED mask check.
Confirmed via strace: the failing /usr/* glob made only one visible syscall, lstat("/usr")=dir
(unrelated); the getattrlist EINVAL is invisible because it returns before issuing any Linux
syscall. Working /* differs because its only component is the magic "*" (no literal component).

FIX (committed): implement both attributes in getattrlist_generic.c.
- xnu submodule: d157c3f "libsystem_kernel: implement getattrlist ATTR_CMN_NAME + ATTR_CMN_OBJTYPE"
- darling parent: 7df268d2d "Update xnu: ..."
ATTR_CMN_NAME packs an attrreference_t + basename (path basename, or /proc/self/fd/<fd> for the
fd variant). ATTR_CMN_OBJTYPE stats the entry and returns the fsobj_type_t. Buffer field order
(name-ref, objtype, then name string in the variable area) matches replace_real_basename.

VALIDATION (libsystem_kernel.dylib rebuilt + deployed to homebrew-test prefix + libexec):
- C getattrlist(path, ATTR_CMN_NAME) now returns "usr"/"bin"/"clang" (was EINVAL).
- Ruby Dir.glob == Dir.entries for /usr/bin (534), /bin (47), Homebrew (225); literal /bin/bash
  => ["/bin/bash"]; /usr/* => 9. Both ruby 4.0.5 and 2.6.3.
- empty_installation? mirror => false (was true) with Pathname.glob listing bin/include/lib.
- FULL from-source build green WITHOUT HOMEBREW_FRESH_KEG_CHECK:
  "brew install --build-from-source lz4" -> lz4 1.10.0, 24 files, built in 28s, binary runs,
  compress/decompress roundtrip OK. (Also moved Homebrew from Tier 3 to Tier 2 - SDK now detected.)

FOLLOW-UPS (separate, not blocking):
- getattrlistbulk still stubbed ENOTSUP; with ATTR_CMN_NAME done it can now be implemented
  (also needs ATTR_CMN_RETURNED_ATTRS). Affects NSDirectoryEnumerator/bulk dir reads.
- Volume case-sensitivity attrs (ATTR_VOL_CAPABILITIES, _PC_CASE_SENSITIVE=11) still EINVAL.
- The glob fix EXPOSED a stale test-prefix SDK symlink (MacOSX10.13.sdk -> MacOSX.sdk while
  SDKSettings.json says 11.3) that tripped Homebrew's check_broken_sdks once all_sdks could be
  globbed. Fixed in the test prefix by replacing it with MacOSX11.sdk. If Darling ships a CLT
  SDK layout, the bundled versioned symlink name must match the SDK's SDKSettings version.

Repro scripts: /tmp/run-gal.sh (getattrlist), /tmp/run-rbvfs.sh (empty_installation?),
/tmp/trace-glob3.sh (strace working-vs-failing diff), /tmp/run-lz4-nohack.sh (end-to-end).

title: Implement getattrlistbulk (was ENOTSUP)
description: Follow-up to dar-e1j. Darling's getattrlistbulk (BSD syscall 461) returned -ENOTSUP, so fts-based tools and NSDirectoryEnumerator silently fell back (or failed). Now implemented in libsystem_kernel.
close_reason:
Implemented in xnu 6159147 / darling 4b504ae86.

getattrlistbulk now iterates the directory via getdents64 and packs each entry in the canonical layout: u32 length, optional ATTR_CMN_RETURNED_ATTRS attribute_set_t, then requested attrs in ascending-bit order. Supported: ATTR_CMN_NAME, OBJTYPE, OBJTAG, FNDRINFO, ERROR, ATTR_DIR_ENTRYCOUNT, ATTR_FILE_RSRCLENGTH. Object type from dirent d_type with fstatat fallback for DT_UNKNOWN. '.'/'..' skipped. Entries that don't fit are left for the next call by rewinding to the last packed entry's d_off cookie via lseek, so iteration spans multiple calls losslessly.

When the caller requests ATTR_CMN_RETURNED_ATTRS (mandatory per man page; fts/NSDirectoryEnumerator do), unsupported attrs are omitted and reported via the returned set instead of EINVAL. Without it, strict fixed-layout + EINVAL on unsupported.

VALIDATED (darling-debug-runner, both prefixes): bulk count == readdir count for /usr/bin (534 across 4 calls -> proves lseek-rewind has no dup/miss), /bin (47), /usr (9), /usr/lib (161/2 calls), /etc (51), / (16), empty dir (0). Object types REG/DIR/LNK correct. Fixed-layout (no RETURNED_ATTRS) path OK. Graceful-omit path: requesting unsupported ATTR_CMN_CRTIME succeeds, bit not set in returned set. Repro: /tmp/run-galbulk.sh, /tmp/run-galbulk2.sh. LOCAL COMMITS ONLY (not pushed).

title: Fix flaky brew install hang: darlingserver interrupt_enter/PUSH_UNKNOWN_REPLIES reply-deferral race
description: CONFIRMED ROOT CAUSE (forkhunt6 iter4, frozen bundle nhs:~/work/darling-debug/forkhunt6-20260608T160728Z/iter4). NOT a deadlock and NOT psynch logic. At the hang darlingserver is IDLE (only 2 OS threads: main in epoll_wait + the single WorkQueue<Thread>::worker parked in pthread_cond_wait = empty work queue; two gdb snapshots 3s apart identical). The stuck ruby thread is blocked in recvmsg awaiting the reply to psynch_mutexdrop(#71), sent immediately after a SIGCHLD (reaped curl child) drove interrupt_enter(#14)/interrupt_exit(#15). => LOST/MISROUTED RPC REPLY during signal handling: the interrupt_enter PUSH_UNKNOWN_REPLIES reply-deferral race explicitly acknowledged in scripts/generate-rpc-wrappers.py:84-101. Homebrew's DownloadQueue forks many curl children -> SIGCHLD storm -> frequent interrupt windows -> a reply (psynch_mutexdrop) is consumed by the wrong recvmsg / lost on push-back -> client recvmsg blocks forever -> download future never completes -> install hangs. Explains: flaky (~1/4); 'brew install' (deps fork storm) hangs but 'brew fetch' doesn't; darlingserver idle; the psynch cvwait validation fix is correct and unrelated. Fix lives in the interrupt_enter/exit + push-reply protocol: darlingserver/src/thread.cpp (pushCallReply ~1361; _interrupts/savedReply/_deferredReply/_pendingSavedReply/_handlingInterruptedCall ~1520-1580) + client generated dserver_rpc_* wrappers (push_reply path, callnum 0xbadca11). RPC wire: first LE32 of each msg = callnum (14=interrupt_enter,15=interrupt_exit,70=cvwait,71=mutexdrop,31=pthread_canceled->-38 ENOSYS benign). Tooling: reproduce with /tmp/forkhunt6.sh on nhs (NEVER pkill -9 darlingserver -> corrupts mount namespace; use darling shutdown).

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: ROOT CAUSE (forkhunt6 iter4): NOT psynch deadlock. darlingserver IDLE at hang (epoll+empty workqueue). Lost/misrouted RPC reply during signal handling: interrupt_enter PUSH_UNKNOWN_REPLIES deferral race (generate-rpc-wrappers.py:84-101). Homebrew curl forks -> SIGCHLD storm -> signal interrupts thread mid-psynch-RPC -> psynch_mutexdrop(#71) reply lost on push-back -> recvmsg blocks forever. Fix target: interrupt_enter/exit + push-reply protocol (mldr client + darlingserver), NOT psynch.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: RE-DIAGNOSIS (from forkhunt6 iter4 client strace, re-read 2026-06-09). PRIOR reply-deferral-race diagnosis is WRONG. Decoded main-thread RPC tail (fd1023): a psynch_mutexdrop(#71) was interrupted by SIGCHLD while blocked on a lock 8.65s; the interrupt_enter(14)/interrupt_exit(15) sequence + the DEFERRED #71 reply all delivered correctly (recv #71 code=0x204 right after interrupt_exit). The HANG is on a SUBSEQUENT, SECOND psynch_mutexdrop(#71) sent ~4ms later with NO signal anywhere near it -> recvmsg never returns. So NOT an interrupt reply-routing loss. Server alive (epoll_wait) + WorkQueue worker in pthread_cond_wait => the managed thread is a microthread-fiber (invisible to gdb) that SUSPENDED on a duct-tape lock/turnstile and never resumed = server-side psynch deadlock / lost wakeup. Hypothesis: resume-during-interrupt of a lock-blocked psynch call (thread.cpp:1523 FIXME 'does not work properly if the thread was suspended waiting for a lock') corrupts psynch internal state (ksyn_wqlock/turnstile waiter), wedging the NEXT psynch_mutexdrop server-side. Fix locus = psynch interrupt/resume + duct-tape lock handling (kern_synch.c + _handleInterruptEnterForCurrentThread), NOT the RPC reply FIFO. Next: reproduce with darlingserver debug logging to pinpoint where the 2nd mutexdrop microthread is stuck.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: ROOT CAUSE CONFIRMED (2026-06-09, instrumented capture forkhuntph-20260609T071914Z/iter1). The Fetching-downloads hang is a LOST MUTEX-HANDOFF WAKEUP in duct-tape psynch: ksyn_wqfind returns DIFFERENT kwq objects for the SAME userspace mutex over time (e.g. mutex 0x7306615095d0 -> kwq 003620 then 026a10 then 026b30 within ~6s). Waiters (psynch_mutexwait) enqueue on one kwq instance; a later psynch_mutexdrop resolves a different kwq whose kw_inqueue does not count the stranded waiter -> FF handoff signals wrong/none -> woken thread re-waits without acquiring -> main + a worker both strand in mutexwait -> RPC reply never sent -> guest blocks in recvmsg -> darlingserver idle (worker parked) -> install hangs. RULED OUT: reply-deferral/Option B (replies all delivered), thread_unblock/TH_WAIT scheduler wakeup (threads ARE rescheduled), network/DNS (skb wait is the unix RPC socket). Fix locus: ksyn_wqfind/ksyn_wqrelease kwq identity+lifecycle in kern_synch.c (same area as is_seqhigher fix, different defect). Full writeup: pr-drafts/darling-psynch-kwq-lost-wakeup-findings.md. Instrumented bin nhs:/tmp/darlingserver.ph2 (md5 58416e), hunter nhs:/tmp/forkhunt-ph.sh (psynch-frozen-90s real-deadlock detector).

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: 2026-06-10 underlying shared-queue root cause refined: kernelAsyncRunnersAvailable is only the victim-visible stale state; the actual lost wakeup is at the duct-tape blocking boundary. Concrete interleaving: kernelAsync worker increments available, semaphore_wait marks its XNU thread TH_WAIT, and thread_block_parameter() observes waiting=true. Before it calls dtape_hooks->thread_suspend()/C++ Thread::suspend(), a producer signals the semaphore. thread_unblock() sets THREAD_AWAKENED and calls Thread::resume(), but resume() sees C++ _suspended=false and returns without scheduling. The signal has already selected/consumed this semaphore waiter, then the worker calls suspend(), sets _suspended=true, and sleeps forever. It never returns from dtape_semaphore_down_simple(), so available remains 1; future producers neither spawn a worker nor get their signaled token consumed. This is a generic check-then-suspend race, not queue locking corruption. The timer fix safely bypasses it, but the common Thread suspend/resume protocol still needs a separate fix.

title: Fix duct-tape Thread suspend/resume lost wakeup
description: Fix the generic lost-wakeup race between duct-tape waits and DarlingServer Thread::suspend()/resume() using a coalesced resume permit. A wake arriving before or during physical suspension is retained until suspend consumes it or doWork safely reschedules the stopped microthread. Restore timer expiry delivery to shared Thread::kernelAsync and remove the dedicated timer workaround.
acceptance_criteria: Amplified wake-before-suspend reproducer fails on baseline and passes with fix; ordinary Ruby timed-wait stress has zero hangs across long runs; heavy timed-wait stress completes with stable resources; timer delivery uses shared kernelAsync; build and Homebrew scenario are validated.
close_reason: Generic suspend/resume lost wakeup fixed and proven; timer workaround removed; amplified and long stress validation passed.

The amplifier has been removed. The generic fix handles both windows: resume before _suspended sets _resumePending, consumed by suspend; resume after _suspended but while _running cancels that in-progress suspend instead of scheduling a concurrently-running microthread.

title: brew install mutex handoff deadlock after cvwait EINTR recovery
description: After the darlingserver suspend/resume lost-wakeup fix (dar-gwn.1.4) the prior psynch/timer hang is gone, but 'brew install' still does not complete end-to-end. It reaches 'Fetching downloads', fetches some files (e.g. *.big_sur.jws.json), then Ruby threads block in socket receive and the run times out at 300s while darlingserver stays healthy (continues servicing timed-wait cycles). Characterized in dar-gwn.1.4 / dar-gwn.1.3 final comments as a NEW network/download stall, distinct from the lost-wakeup hang. NOT yet diagnosed. Next: fresh 'brew install --force-bottle wget' on nhs against current darlingserver 26f50b5 to capture the exact stall point (socket fd, server-side state).
design:
ROOT CAUSE (confirmed 2026-06-10, repro 20260610T115553Z-brew-fetch-rpcattach):
brew-fetch hang = LOST psynch condition-variable wakeup, NOT network / suspend-resume / SIOCGIFCONF.

WHAT IS CLEAR:
- Orphan RPC behind the client recvmsg hang = psynch_cvwait (callnum 71). ruby main thread sends cvwait, server parks it in the ksyn waitq, and thread_unblock is NEVER called for it => no RPC reply => recvmsg blocks forever.
- Proven by host strace (--attach-strace) of ruby pid 430228 correlated with dserver.log [PH] trace: main thread dt=0x70f5c4033a90 final event BLOCK cont=psynch_cvcontinue @736.305; zero subsequent UNBLOCK for that dt in the whole 1.7GB log. A 2nd worker (tid 248) also stuck in cvwait.
- cv 0x79c56a6048e0 is producer/consumer: worker threads cvsignal (callnum 70) to wake main. Last cvsignal @722.195, BEFORE cvwait #1 (722.289) and #2 (736.304). cvwait #1 escaped only via SIGCHLD (ERESTARTSYS); cvwait #2 had no signal and hung.
- The lost wakeup stands immediately after a SIGCHLD-interrupted (ALLOW_INTERRUPTIONS) cvwait #1 => suspect: prepost/sequence (sword/lword/cgen/csgen) mis-accounted when a cvwait leaves the queue via interrupt rather than via signal, so a later signal is absorbed / the final waiter is left unmatched.
- NOT dar-q95.8 (suspend/resume): no UNBLOCK was ever issued, nothing lost at resume level. q95.1 only relaxed cvwait validation (is_seqhigher_eq->is_seqhigher); does not fix this.

PLAN (option B): add targeted [CVDBG] logging (cv, sword/lword, cgen/csgen, nwaiters, kwe_state, interrupted-vs-signaled) in __psynch_cvsignal / ksyn_cvsignal / ksyn_handle_cvbroad / ksyn_wait / psynch_cvcontinue / _kwq_mark_interruped_wakeup / _kwq_handle_interrupted_wakeup; disable the [PH] firehose; reproduce once and pin the exact sequence desync, then targeted fix.

Repro: darling-debug-runner darling --attach-strace 'brew\.rb fetch' -- bash -c 'brew fetch wget' (needs ptrace_scope=0). File: src/external/darlingserver/duct-tape/pthread/kern_synch.c.
close_reason: All 3 psynch deadlock layers fixed and validated (brew fetch wget 13/13; brew install jq no longer hangs). Layer 3 (libpthread negative-error decode for cond/mutex/rwlock) committed: libpthread 3de6464, xnu 0a10e19, parent f510f71. Remaining jq failure is an unrelated CLT/bottle env limitation.

The earlier cvwait #1 (722.289) blocked 14s and was released only by SIGCHLD (ERESTARTSYS), not by a signal.
- dserver.log [PH] trace (same run, pid 430228): main thread dt=0x70f5c4033a90 final event = BLOCK cont=psynch_cvcontinue at 736.305006; ZERO subsequent 'UNBLOCK dt=0x70f5c4033a90' anywhere in the 1.7GB log (any executor). comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
ROOT CAUSE PINNED via [CVDBG] (stall reproduced 1/6 runs without strace, dserver-stall-iter1.log, 223 lines). Heisenbug: disabling the [PH] firehose narrowed but did NOT remove the race. Stuck cv=0x7a9908209470 (consumer tid nstid=23; producers 174/175/220/221). Sequence (PTHRW_INC=0x100, low byte=flags):

gen 0x100/0x200: healthy cvwait->cvsignal->cvcontinue(err=0).
gen 0x300: cvwait INTERRUPTED -> cvcontinue result=2(THREAD_INTERRUPTED) error=4(EINTR). error-branch: removes own kwe, sword 0x201+=PTHRW_INC=0x301, (lword 0x300 & MASK)==(sword 0x301 & MASK)=0x300 -> ECVCLEARED -> ZEROES cv (lword=uword=sword=0, KSYN_KWF_ZEROEDOUT). final_error=260=EINTR|ECVCLEARED.
In-flight signal tid220 (cgen=0x300,csgen=0x200) hits the zeroed cv: UPDATE_CVKWQ(ZEROEDOUT) reinits sword=rw_wc=csgen=0x200 -- REGRESSES sword from 0x300 to 0x200. No waiter present -> becomes PREPOST@0x300 (fakecount=1,inqueue=1), updatebits=0, sword stays 0x200.
gen 0x400: tid23 re-waits lockseq=0x400. find_cvpreposeq(0x400) returns NULL because it requires kwe_lockseq>=cgen and the prepost is at 0x300<0x400 -> prepost stranded forever. tid23 BLOCKS (inqueue=2). A real signal (tid221, uptoseq 0x400) wakes it, sword 0x200->0x300, prepost@0x300 remains.
gen 0x500: interrupted again -> EINTR|ECVPREPOST(516); fakecount==inqueue.
gen 0x600: tid23 blocks (csgen==lockseq==0x600, allowed by q95.1 relaxation). NO producer signals 0x600 -> permanent block. Whole process quiesces (all worker threads idle/blocked). Same shape as the original cv 0x79c56a6048e0 evidence.

MECHANISM: a Unix-signal (SIGCHLD from the fetch child) interrupting cvwait (THREAD_ABORTSAFE) at generation N races with the producer's in-flight cvsignal for N. The ECVCLEARED zero-out + the signal's UPDATE_CVKWQ reinit-from-csgen regresses kw_sword by one PTHRW_INC, losing one signal generation. The orphaned prepost can never be consumed (find_cvpreposeq seq>=cgen). NOT suspend/resume (q95.8): the dtape wakeup primitives all returned kr=0; this is pure psynch sequence-accounting corruption in kern_synch.c. NEXT: compare cvcontinue interrupt path + UPDATE_CVKWQ ZEROEDOUT against upstream Apple libpthread to locate the exact deviation.

Rationale: ksyn_signal + shim_wait_wakeup (kr=0) already prove the wakeup primitive fired and thread_unblock requested a resume for ALL psynch types; the only remaining unknown for a stuck thread is whether its continuation actually executed. comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
Round-2 result (dserver-stall-iter1.log, 143 lines, stall again on iter 1): RULED OUT lost microthread resume / suspend-resume (dar-q95.8). The ONLY stuck thread was nstid=23 in cvwait (tstore=nil, block_hint=14); every other thread completed its mtxcontinue (resume delivered fine) or returned to userspace. So this is PURE psynch sequence-accounting in kern_synch.c, not the scheduler.

Clean full trace of stuck cv=0x7e48c7f093d0 (consumer tid23) shows orphan-prepost ACCUMULATION:
- gen 0x100 cvwait INTERRUPTED (EINTR) before any signal -> waiter removes self, ECVCLEARED, cv zeroed. Late signal for 0x100 lands on zeroed cv -> orphan PREPOST@0x100 (fakecount=1).
- gen 0x200: real signal wakes it (err=0), but orphan@0x100 stays (inqueue=1).
- gen 0x300 INTERRUPTED again -> EINTR|ECVPREPOST(516); now 1 orphan. sword lags lword.
- gen 0x400: cvwait_lookup shows inqueue=2 fakecount=2 (TWO orphan preposts) -> blocks; no further signal arrives -> permanent block. find_cvpreposeq never matches orphans (requires kwe_lockseq>=cgen; orphans are at lower seqs).

ERROR-RETURN PLUMBING: psynch_cvcontinue returns the AUGMENTED error via unix_syscall_return -> thread_syscall_return -> Thread::syscallReturn -> Call::sendBSDReply(resultCode=error, returnValue=_bsdReturnValue). For the interrupt case error=516=EINTR(4)|ECVPREPOST(0x200) (ECVCLEARED=0x100, ECVPREPOST=0x200 per kern_internal.h). If the client maps resultCode straight to errno, errno=516 != EINTR(4) -> libpthread EINTR/prepost-cleanup logic may not fire. Observed: after EINTR the consumer goes straight to the next cvwait ~1ms later with NO _psynch_cvclrprepost call in between -> orphan preposts are never cleaned. _psynch_cvclrprepost IS wired (kern_init + RPC callnum + cvar branch ksyn_queue_free_items) but appears uninvoked. Round-3 (cvclrprepost_enter instrumentation) running to confirm whether libpthread ever calls it.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: Round-3 CONFIRMED: _psynch_cvclrprepost is called ZERO times during the entire hang (added cvclrprepost_enter [CVDBG], stall reproduced again on iter 1, grep count = 0). So userspace libpthread never cleans the orphan preposts. The starvation is real: the producer signaled N times; each signal that landed on an interrupted (absent) waiter became an orphan prepost (a genuinely LOST wakeup, not just leaked memory), so later legitimate waiters get fewer signals than waits and one starves forever. Note: condvars do NOT use the kw_intr interrupted-wakeup redirection mechanism that mutex/rwlock use (_kwq_mark/_handle_interrupted_wakeup are KWQ_INTR_WRITE only) -- so an interrupt-vs-signal race on a cv has no redirection safety net. Next: diff darling kern_synch.c (_psynch_cvwait prepost scan, psynch_cvcontinue interrupt path, ksyn_queue_find_cvpreposeq) against upstream Apple libpthread to find the exact port deviation before choosing a fix.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
ROOT CAUSE FOUND + FIX APPLIED.

Upstream diff: darling's duct-tape/pthread/kern_synch.c (_psynch_cvwait, psynch_cvcontinue, ksyn_queue_find_cvpreposeq, kw_intr) is a FAITHFUL byte-for-byte port of Apple libpthread-454.120.2 kern/kern_synch.c -- no kernel-side deviation. Real macOS doesn't deadlock because userspace libpthread (_pthread_psynch_cond_wait in src/pthread_cond.c) handles the cvwait error: 'if (updateval == (uint32_t)-1) { err=errno; switch(err & 0xff){case EINTR:...} _pthread_cond_updateval(cond,mutex,err,0); }' where _pthread_cond_updateval decodes err & ECVCLEARED / err & ECVPREPOST and triggers __psynch_cvclrprepost. The trigger REQUIRES the syscall to return -1 with errno = the augmented error.

THE BUG (darling client glue, NOT the kernel): src/external/xnu/darling/src/libsystem_kernel/emulation/src/xnu_syscall/bsd/impl/psynch/psynch_cvwait.c, sys_psynch_cvwait did 'return (ret) ? ret : retval;' where ret = dserver_rpc header.code = the BSD error from psynch_cvcontinue (e.g. 516 = EINTR|ECVPREPOST, 260 = EINTR|ECVCLEARED). Returning it POSITIVELY made the macOS syscall look SUCCESSFUL with updateval=516, so libpthread took the success branch: never saw EINTR, never called _pthread_cond_updateval, never called psynch_cvclrprepost. The orphan prepost from the interrupted cvwait was thus never cleared -> accumulated -> sword lagged lword -> a later legitimate waiter starved -> permanent condvar block. Intermittent because cvwait only returns an error on the rare SIGCHLD-interrupt race.

FIX: 'return (ret) ? -ret : retval;' -- propagate the cv error as a negative value so cerror sets errno (full 516, high bits intact) and the syscall returns -1; libpthread then masks errno&0xff=EINTR and inspects ECVCLEARED/ECVPREPOST -> calls cvclrprepost. ret is the header.code which is non-zero ONLY on error (cv updateval bits travel via the separate retval/uthread_set_returnval), so -ret is always correct. Built libsystem_kernel.dylib, installed into darling-prefix/libexec/darling/usr/lib/system/. Validating with 6x brew fetch wget loop (was hanging on iter 1 every prior round). NOTE: sibling waiters psynch_mutexwait.c / psynch_rw_wrlock.c / psynch_rw_rdlock.c have the identical 'return (ret)?ret:retval' pattern and the same latent bug for their EINTR/ETIMEDOUT returns -- candidates for the same one-line fix (mutex/rwlock are partly shielded by the kw_intr redirection mechanism that condvars lack).

comments.text:
FIX VALIDATION round 2 + RESIDUAL IS A DIFFERENT BUG.

Confirmed the errno plumbing is correct: darling SYS.h x86_64 DARLING macro does 'call __darling_bsd_syscall; cmpq $-4095,%rax; (in error range) negq %rdi; call _cerror' -- the negative return becomes errno = -rax with NO truncation and NO Linux->BSD table conversion, so the full augmented value (516/316 incl. ECVCLEARED/ECVPREPOST bits) reaches libpthread. cvclrprepost staying at 0 is NOT a bug: libpthread only calls it when its 'needclearpre' drain condition triggers, which is rare. The psynch -ret fix is therefore correct and complete for its bug class (orphan prepost from interrupted cvwait): fixed runs show NO orphan accumulation.

The psynch fix dramatically reduced hangs (was 100% on iter 1; now passes multiple iters) but a RESIDUAL hang remains, and gdb + [CVDBG] prove it is a SEPARATE bug:
- gdb of the hung brew process: main thread blocked in recvmsg on the dserver RPC socket (a stuck psynch wait); a sibling thread in wait4(child) and another in select() -- i.e. brew is running an external download command (curl) and the main Ruby thread waits on a condvar for it.
- dserver [CVDBG]: stuck cv 0x773643409450, consumer tid23. gen 0x300: cvwait_block with abstime=0 / deadline=0 (a plain pthread_cond_WAIT, no timeout, sec=0 nsec=0). After ~29s it received cvcontinue result=1 (THREAD_TIMED_OUT) error=60(ETIMEDOUT) -- a SPURIOUS timeout on a no-deadline wait. That zeroed the cv (ECVCLEARED), the consumer re-waited at gen 0x400, the producer never signals it -> permanent block.

ROOT of residual = a spurious THREAD_TIMED_OUT delivered to a deadline=0 (wait-forever) psynch wait in duct-tape, NOT psynch sequence accounting. This matches the in-tree 'timer lost-wakeup reproducer' work (commits 7b266b7f7 / 7288e6305 / 6b682e086) and is dar-q95.8 (suspend/resume / timer) territory, a distinct issue from dar-gwn.2's psynch error-return bug. comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: RESIDUAL ROOT CAUSE FOUND + FIX (darlingserver, separate from the psynch errno fix). The spurious THREAD_TIMED_OUT on a deadline=0 wait is a MISSING WAIT-TIMER CANCELLATION in duct-tape. waitq_assert_wait64_locked (duct-tape/xnu/osfmk/kern/waitq.c:2926-2928) arms thread->wait_timer (wait_timer_is_set=TRUE, wait_timer_active++) for a timed wait. XNU's thread_unblock() (osfmk/kern/sched_prim.c:631-635) cancels it on wakeup, but duct-tape's thread_unblock (duct-tape/src/thread.c) did NOT -- it only cancelled in dtape_thread_destroy(). So a timed wait woken EARLY (by signal or a normal condvar/mutex wakeup before its deadline) leaks an armed wait_timer; it fires later in thread_timer_expire() -> clear_wait_internal(THREAD_TIMED_OUT), delivering a spurious timeout to whatever the thread is doing then. Observed: tid23 main brew thread, gen 0x300 plain pthread_cond_WAIT (abstime=0, deadline=0) received cvcontinue result=1 (THREAD_TIMED_OUT) error=60 ~29s after blocking -> ECVCLEARED zeroed the cv -> re-wait gen 0x400 never signalled -> permanent hang while a child download (wait4/select threads) was in flight. Intermittent because it needs a timed wait woken early followed by a critical untimed wait, and most condvar waits are untimed (no timer). FIX: add the XNU wait_timer cancel block to duct-tape thread_unblock (timer_call_cancel + wait_timer_active-- + wait_timer_is_set=FALSE). Safe re-entrancy: thread_timer_expire sets wait_timer_is_set=FALSE before clear_wait_internal->thread_go->thread_unblock, so the timer-expiry path skips the cancel. Distinct from dar-gwn.1.3 (lost timer DELIVERY) and dar-q95.8 (resume race); this is spurious timer DELIVERY-after-cancel-miss. Rebuilding + validating.

2026-06-10 layer-3 correction after targeted mutex instrumentation and fresh post-fix reproductions:

The alleged mutex hand-off deadlock is DISPROVEN. Added [CVDBG] around _psynch_mutexdrop_internal, _psynch_mutexwait, ksyn_mtxsignal, and psynch_mtxcontinue. In both fresh timeout traces, every mutex waiter received a matching drop/grant and mtxcontinue; no mutex owner or waiter was stranded. The earlier two-waiter terminal snapshot was transient and was incorrectly classified without drop/handoff visibility.

Fresh post-L1+L2 runs also show clean condition-variable accounting: no orphan preposts, no spurious THREAD_TIMED_OUT, and no balanced cgen==csgen entry. The only balanced entry remains in the old pre-fix SAVED trace and arose downstream of the already-fixed error/timer corruption. A speculative balanced-wait immediate-return patch was tested locally, did not trigger, and was REMOVED rather than masking an invalid state.

Current 120s timeout signature is a separate real child-command stall: live process tree shows brew Ruby waiting while /usr/bin/curl --silent --head https://ftpmirror.gnu.org/gnu/wget/wget-1.25.0.tar.gz remains blocked in do_select for the full timeout. Host curl to the same URL returns HTTP 302 in ~2s. darlingserver remains healthy, mutex handoffs complete, and the final Ruby condvar wait is clean/unsignaled because the curl child has not exited. Tracked separately as a child of dar-gwn.

Conclusion: dar-gwn.2 psynch/timer race is resolved by committed layers 1 and 2: xnu f3c8d94 / parent f38d2a146 and darlingserver 32af4d5 / parent f6f5fba31. The expanded [CVDBG] instrumentation remains intentionally uncommitted in darlingserver for future diagnosis.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: 2026-06-10 E2E REPRO CONFIRMS LAYER 3 IS REAL. Command: brew install jq under current L1+L2 fixed build. Bundle in progress: /home/ilyagulya/work/darling-debug/20260610T170420Z-brew-install-jq-e2e. After fetching, no live curl/compiler remains; Ruby main + system_command threads block in dserver RPC recvmsg; darlingserver is idle (epoll + empty worker). Final [CVDBG] sequence on mutex 0x7699d15095d0: thread225 drops mutex from cvwait and grants thread226 (0x403); thread226 later drops and grants main tid23 (0x503); main mtxcontinue succeeds, then immediately mutexwaits at 0x602 while owner hint resolves to thread225; thread226 then mutexwaits at 0x702 with same owner thread225. Thread225 is itself sleeping in cvwait 0x7699798adc10 after having dropped this mutex. No owner drops again, so both waiters strand. This directly restores the previously suspected cvwait-EINTR|ECVPREPOST -> mutex reacquire/handoff layer. Earlier fresh brew-fetch traces only disproved it for those particular fetch-only runs; closing dar-gwn.2 was premature. Next: determine why userspace owner TID points back to cv-waiting thread225 after grants to thread226/main.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: Reopened: Reopened after true end-to-end brew install jq validation reproduced the layer-3 mutex deadlock. Earlier fetch-only runs did not exercise this path.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: 2026-06-10 live E2E diagnosis update (brew install jq): corrected the initial mutex-deadlock interpretation. Mutex handoffs themselves complete: thread225 cvwait drops/grants thread226; thread226 drops/grants main; both mtxcontinue callbacks run. The apparent owner thread225 is not asleep: its cvwait is signaled and cvcontinue completes, then it reacquires the mutex. It subsequently busy-spins while holding the mutex, so main/system_command correctly block behind it. Host strace over 5s: thread225 performs ~34,000 sched_yield calls; companion workqueue thread cycles epoll_wait(10ms)/clock_gettime/sysinfo. Live memory pins the busy-loop to condvar 0x7699d1509490: L=0x300, U=0x400, S=0x200 (U > L, invalid). libpthread _pthread_psynch_cond_signal explicitly loops with sched_yield while U > L, explaining the spin and retained mutex. This invalid state appears immediately after main cvwait returns EINTR|ECVPREPOST (final_error=516): trace before terminal state has orphan fake prepost and L/S lag. Therefore layer 3 is not a kernel mutex handoff failure; it is userspace condvar U/S accounting corruption after interrupted cvwait recovery, causing pthread_cond_signal to spin forever while owning the associated mutex. Artifacts: /home/ilyagulya/work/darling-debug/brew-install-jq-live-gdb.txt, brew-install-jq-cv-memory.txt, brew-install-jq-busy-thread-strace.log. Next: trace _pthread_cond_updateval / signal-side U transitions or compare installed libpthread behavior to expected error recovery to identify why U advances past L.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: CORRECTION to preceding live-memory interpretation: COND_GETSEQ_ADDR for aligned condvars maps c_seq[0]=L, c_seq[1]=S, c_seq[2]=U. Terminal memory at 0x7699d1509490 is therefore L=0x300, S=0x400, U=0x200, not U=0x400/S=0x200. The busy-loop is _pthread_psynch_cond_signal first validation branch (S > L), not U > L. This points to DOUBLE/EXCESS accounting of the interrupted waiter on userspace S after final_error=516. At cvwait entry userspace passed csgen=0x202 (S count 0x200); one correct EINTR|ECVPREPOST _pthread_cond_updateval should advance S only to 0x300, balanced with L. Actual S=0x400 means the same wait was accounted at least twice. Next instrumentation target: userspace _pthread_cond_updateval call count/error/updateval and cancellation cleanup path.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: 2026-06-10 CONDDBG E2E update: bundle /home/ilyagulya/work/darling-debug/20260610T171818Z-brew-install-jq-conddbg timed out, but it disproves double userspace updateval accounting. For cv 0x7bfd69e09460, _pthread_cond_updateval is called ONCE at the failing transition with error=0/updateval=0x204; it advances S 0x400 -> 0x600 while L=0x500, producing S>L and the signal-side sched_yield spin. Server CVDBG for the same wait reports cvcontinue_exit final_error=516 (0x204 = EINTR|ECVPREPOST). Therefore the immediate cause is that this run sees 0x204 as a successful syscall retval instead of errno, exactly the pre-L1 positive-BSD-error behavior. Strong suspicion: test DPREFIX has a stale libsystem_kernel.dylib shadowing the fixed main-prefix dylib. Next: compare/install fixed L1 dylib into DPREFIX and rerun true E2E.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: 2026-06-10 control E2E after installing fixed-L1 dylib into test DPREFIX: STILL TIMEOUT, bundle /home/ilyagulya/work/darling-debug/20260610T172330Z-brew-install-jq-fixed-kernel-e2e. Important correction: stale-positive 0x204 is gone, but the fixed wrapper now exposes another ABI mismatch. CONDDBG repeatedly sees error=0 with updateval=0xfffffffc (-EINTR), 0xfffffdfc (-516), etc. _pthread_psynch_cond_wait only treats updateval==(uint32_t)-1 as syscall error and otherwise feeds any nonzero value into _pthread_cond_updateval as a successful wake; thus negative BSD errors corrupt S in the opposite direction and the install still hangs. Server logs final_error=516, while userspace receives updateval=-516 rather than -1 with errno=516. Conclusion: L1 change from positive ret to -ret is insufficient/wrong at this libpthread ABI boundary; wrapper must produce libc syscall-error semantics (-1 + errno), or libpthread must explicitly decode negative returns. brew does NOT work E2E yet. Next: inspect generated psynch syscall wrapper/error conversion and fix the boundary, then rerun.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: 2026-06-10 root cause of failed L1 confirmed in source: sys_psynch_* implementations are called directly from the BSD syscall table; returning -ret does NOT invoke cerror automatically. Patched cvwait/mutexwait/rw_rdlock/rw_wrlock wrappers to call cerror(ret) explicitly, which should yield the ABI libpthread expects: return -1 and errno=BSD error/status bits. Build of libsystem_kernel.dylib succeeds. Installing into main + Homebrew test prefixes, then rerunning E2E.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: 2026-06-10 explicit-cerror trial result: build succeeded but brew install jq failed fast with Ruby pthread_cond_wait EINVAL; bundle /home/ilyagulya/work/darling-debug/20260610T173038Z-brew-install-jq-cerror-e2e. CONDDBG shows the failing cvwait reaches updateval with error=0x1, not the expected composite 0x104/0x204. So a plain C call cerror(ret) is also not preserving the composite psynch status errno correctly in this emulation path. This trial is diagnostic only; brew still does not work. Investigating cerror implementation/calling convention before choosing the boundary fix.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
LAYER 3 ROOT CAUSE FOUND (static + CONDDBG evidence) + FIX IN PROGRESS.

The neighboring agent's discovery overturns the 14:02 beads conclusion that 'errno plumbing is correct': for these EMULATED BSD psynch syscalls the negative BSD-error return does NOT pass through cerror/errno. Proven by [CONDDBG] in libpthread _pthread_cond_updateval: the committed -ret build showed updateval=0xffffffc4 (=-60=-ETIMEDOUT) arriving DIRECTLY as updateval with error=0x0 -> libpthread misread the negative error as a giant successful updateval. The neighboring agent's libpthread decode change ((int32_t)updateval<0 -> err=-updateval) in _pthread_psynch_cond_wait fixes the CONDVAR path: negative-decode run shows error=0x13c (ETIMEDOUT|ECVCLEARED) decoded correctly. That decode fix is CONFIRMED GOOD and must be kept.

LAYER 3 = SAME bug class, unpatched on the MUTEX and RWLOCK consumer paths:
- src/pthread_mutex.c:773 (fairshare) and :1415 (firstfit): 'while (updateval == (uint32_t)-1)'. With emulation returning -EINTR (0xfffffffc) directly, != 0xffffffff -> loop EXITS treating interrupted mutex wait as SUCCESSFUL acquisition -> stuck handoff. Exactly the 14:57 layer-3 signature (tid23+tid221 parked in psynch_mutexwait, no owner drops it). Kernel kw_intr redirection only works if userspace RE-ENTERS the wait; broken ==-1 loop never retries.
- src/pthread_rwlock.c:644: relies on errno (never set here) -> same failure; PTHREAD_INTERNAL_CRASHes on other decoded errors.

FIX: mutex/rwlock retry loops treat negative BSD-error return as 'interrupted, retry' (mirror validated cond decode (int32_t)updateval<0). Implementing now; rebuild + validate via brew install jq e2e and cvdbg-loop.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
BREAKTHROUGH — LAYER 3 FIX VALIDATED, psynch deadlock RESOLVED.

Applied the layer-3 fix in libpthread (mutex/rwlock consumer paths must treat the directly-returned negative BSD error as 'interrupted -> retry the kernel wait', mirroring the validated cond decode):
- src/pthread_mutex.c:773 (fairshare) and the firstfit loop: 'while (updateval == (uint32_t)-1)' -> 'while ((int32_t)updateval < 0)'.
- src/pthread_rwlock.c:644: added an '(int32_t)updateval < 0 -> res = -updateval' branch before the success branch so EINTR retries instead of being mistaken for a successful rwlock updateval.

VALIDATION (libsystem_pthread.dylib rebuilt 8530bfbb, installed to darling-prefix + darling-prefix-homebrew-test):
- brew fetch wget cvdbg-loop: 8/8 iters RESULT=exited. Before any layer-3 fix this stalled on iter 1-2 with 100% reliability.
- brew install jq e2e (bundle 20260610T192658Z-brew-install-jq-layer3-fix-e2e): RESULT=failed (NOT timeout). The process now gets PAST 'Fetching downloads' (previously the permanent hang point) — it downloaded the automake/libtool/oniguruma dependency formulae — then terminated normally at a Homebrew toolchain error: 'Your Command Line Tools (CLT) does not support macOS 11' (jq has no bottle on macOS 11 Tier 3 -> brew attempted a source build needing CLT). That CLT/bottle limitation is an UNRELATED Homebrew environment issue, not a psynch hang.

CONCLUSION: all 3 layers of dar-gwn.2 are fixed:
  (1) psynch wait wrappers return BSD errors as -ret [xnu f3c8d94 / parent f38d2a146].
  (2) duct-tape thread_unblock cancels wait_timer (spurious THREAD_TIMED_OUT) [darlingserver 32af4d5 / parent f6f5fba31].
  (3) libpthread cond/mutex/rwlock consumers DECODE the directly-returned negative BSD error (cerror/errno does NOT fire for these emulated BSD syscalls) so EINTR retries / prepost cleanup fires [libpthread, this change — cond decode from the neighboring agent + mutex/rwlock decode added now].
Finalizing: stripping [CONDDBG] debug, correcting the now-inaccurate 'cerror sets errno' wrapper comments, rebuild+commit locally.

title: Upgrade CLT to ld64>=507 so -oso_prefix works (drop Homebrew workaround)
description:
Replace the Xcode-9-era CLT (ld64-305, no -oso_prefix) in the Homebrew test prefix with one new enough to accept -oso_prefix, removing the need for the 3-edit Homebrew oso_prefix gate (a local workaround, NOT an upstream candidate — see dar-pk4 analysis).

MINIMUM: CLT 11.0 / Xcode 11.0 = ld64-507 (first release with -oso_prefix). Version map: Xcode9=ld64-305 (current,broken), Xcode10=351, Xcode11.0=507, Xcode12=609, Xcode13.2=711. Tradeoff: newer clears -oso_prefix more safely but raises emulation risk (newer clang/ld need newer dyld/libsystem features Darling may not fully emulate); CLT 11 is closest-to-working-old while still >=507.

ALREADY ON DISK (host nhs, /home/ilyagulya/work):
- Command_Line_Tools_for_Xcode_13.2.pkg (591MB) — ld64-711, exactly the version Homebrew's own build warning requests ('download the Command Line Tools for Xcode 13.2.1').
- darling-prefix-homebrew-clt13/ + .workdir — a PRIOR attempt with CLT 13.2 already extracted; its ld is verified ld64-711. But the working test prefix STILL uses ld64-305, strongly implying the clt13 attempt hit emulation gaps and was not adopted (this is why we remained on the old CLT).
- darling-clt-pkgs/ — the current old CLT (CLTools_Executables.pkg + CLTools_SDK_macOS1013.pkg).
- clt-{deep-trace,sequence-case,version-plain}.rb — version-probing scripts from the prior attempt.

NEXT STEP (cheap before any reinstall): diagnose why darling-prefix-homebrew-clt13 was not adopted — build lz4 from source in it on ld64-711/clang-1300 and capture where it stalls. If it hits a specific emulation hole, decide fix-vs-fallback-to-CLT-11. Alternative track: option (A) in dar-pk4 — use Darling's own cctools ld64 with CLT clang via -fuse-ld, avoiding a CLT swap entirely.

NOTE: dar-e1j (getattrlist), getattrlistbulk, SDK symlink rename (3c79b03fa), and sandbox-exec stub (f85f220f0) are all DONE; -oso_prefix is the last remaining build-toolchain blocker for out-of-box source builds.
close_reason: CLT 13.2 (clang-1300/ld64-711) installed into homebrew-test via Darling's native installer; toolchain + Homebrew version-floor + -oso_prefix all validated; lz4 --build-from-source green (5/6). CLT-upgrade variable closed-success and the -oso_prefix gate is now a no-op on ld64-711. CLT 11.5 ruled out (fails Homebrew's macOS-11 floor >=12.5). Residual blocker for RELIABLE out-of-box builds is the intermittent (~17%) psynch/kwq lost-wakeup flake -> tracked in dar-gwn.1 (not a CLT issue).

title: Fix mldr raw-fork child deadlock on inherited glibc _rtld_global lock
description: After the SA_RESTART/Ruby blocking-read hang was fixed, full lz4 reproduction exposed a separate pre-exec child deadlock. Capture: /home/ilyagulya/work/darling-debug/20260611T091302Z-lz4-sarestart-fix, child PID 3144244. GDB stack: futex_wait(_rtld_global+4344) -> __lll_lock_wait_private -> get_cached_stack -> allocate_stack -> __pthread_create_2_1 -> __darling_thread_create (src/startup/mldr/elfcalls/threads.c:196). Darling raw Linux fork bypasses glibc child lock fixups; child later creates a native mldr thread and deadlocks on inherited glibc stack-cache/loader state. Calling full native glibc fork was tested and rejected: it runs native callbacks and breaks launchd/bootstrap (bundle /home/ilyagulya/work/darling-debug/20260611T091902Z-fork-native-regressions). Find a fix that preserves Darling fork semantics while preventing/recovering inherited mldr/glibc locks.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: Investigated glibc _Fork as a no-atfork replacement. Rejected before deployment: host _Fork@@GLIBC_2.34 disassembly is essentially raw clone plus robust-list reset, and glibc source shows only full __libc_fork calls reclaim_stacks()/resets loader locks. Therefore _Fork cannot repair the captured inherited dl_stack_cache_lock. Testing a narrower mldr fork lock that serializes raw fork with native pthread_create without running callbacks.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: Narrow mldr fork-lock experiment rejected and reverted. It serialized raw fork against native pthread_attr/pthread_create and reset its own lock in the child, preserving bootstrap and passing a 20-task fork smoke. However the main lz4 series still reproduced: /home/ilyagulya/work/darling-debug/20260611T093042Z-lz4flaky, runs 1-2 PASS, run 3 HANG after Formula lz4 before make. Therefore the inherited glibc lock window is broader than pthread_create alone (thread exit/stack reclaim and/or loader state); do not use this narrow serialization as the fix.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
=== dar-gwn.5 FULL STATE + HANDOFF (consolidated; earlier comments were lost to a br syntax error) ===

ROOT CAUSE (confirmed via gdb capture, child 3144244): after Darling's raw Linux fork, the child deadlocks in pthread_create -> allocate_stack -> get_cached_stack, blocked on glibc's GL(_dl_stack_cache_lock) (= _rtld_global+4344 on host glibc 2.39), inherited HELD because a parent native thread held it at fork time. __darling_thread_create backs every Darwin thread with a native pthread, so the child wedges before spawning any subprocess. This IS the flaky lz4/brew --build-from-source hang.

OPTIONS EVALUATED (all empirically tested with a RELIABLE shared-lib harness; do not re-tread):
- A METHODOLOGY TRAP: referencing _rtld_global with `extern` from an EXECUTABLE emits R_X86_64_COPY that snapshots ld.so's live loader state into BSS and desyncs it -> breaks ALL threading (even baseline). Must use a shared lib (PIC) OR dlsym at runtime.
- B (user-provided native stacks via pthread_attr_setstack to bypass get_cached_stack): INVALID. glibc takes _dl_stack_cache_lock to list_add the new thread to _dl_stack_used on the user-stack path too. Proven: poke lock held -> user-stack pthread_create still hangs.
- C (full native fork()/_Fork so glibc resets it): INVALID on glibc 2.34+. Proven: with lock held, BOTH fork() and _Fork() children still hang -> neither resets _dl_stack_cache_lock. Also full fork() separately breaks Darling's dserver child-checkin (bundle 20260611T091902Z: pid=1 launchd aborts on dserver_rpc code=-32). The atfork fear was unfounded (mldr links only -lrt -ldl -lm; Darwin atfork handlers run in emulated libc, not native fork scope).
- A (explicit child-side reset): WORKS. Proven: child sets lock=0 -> OK, +20 threads after -> OK. Discovery of the offset via startup layout signature finds 4344 reliably.

FIX IMPLEMENTED (Option A) - UNCOMMITTED working-tree change in src/startup/mldr/mldr.c (+77 lines):
- __mldr_detect_stack_cache_lock(): base = dlsym(RTLD_DEFAULT,"_rtld_global") (NOT extern - copy-reloc trap). Scan rtld_global for the stack-list signature: list_t _dl_stack_used (empty self-ref), list_t _dl_stack_user (1 elem = main thread), list_t _dl_stack_cache (empty self-ref), size_t actsize=0, uintptr in_flight=0, int lock=0. Store &lock and &cache. No hardcoded glibc offset; safe no-op if signature not found. Called once near top of main() while single-threaded.
- __mldr_postfork_child(): if located, empty _dl_stack_cache (self-ref), zero actsize + in_flight, then __atomic_store_n(lock,0) LAST. Runs in fork child before any thread create (sys_fork child path calls it via elfcalls vtable).

BUILD/DEPLOY:
- cd ~/work/darling-build && ninja src/startup/mldr/all   (mldr + mldr32, OK; only pre-existing stack.c warnings)
- cp built mldr,mldr32 -> ~/work/darling-prefix/libexec/darling/usr/libexec/darling/   (DEPLOYED; mldr md5 86ab0f85...)
- SAFETY: readelf -r on built mldr shows NO R_X86_64_COPY for _rtld_global (dlsym-only). Smoke: darling shell boot + fork/exec OK.

VALIDATION:
- lz4 round1 (MAKE_JOBS=1, bundle 20260611T104715Z-lz4flaky): runs 1-5 PASS, 0 HANG; run6 cut by the 800s runner timeout (not a failure).

OPTION B DIAGNOSIS (source builds / CLT) — exact blocker found. The fatal 'Your CLT does not support macOS 11' comes from extend/os/mac/diagnostic.rb:464 check_if_supported_sdk_available: fires when DevelopmentTools.installed? To enable source builds: provide an SDK with SDKSettings.json Version>=11 that Homebrew can scan (e.g. add SDKSettings.json to MacOSX.sdk + a MacOSX11.sdk dir/symlink), accepting that headers are 10.13 so some builds may still fail. Two stashed CLTs exist (CommandLineTools.apple-clt-15.3.* and .incompatible.*) — candidates to evaluate. Not done (Option A satisfies the request); track here if full source-build support is wanted.

Toolchain is fine: CLT clang (Apple LLVM 9.0.0 / clang-900) and ld64-305 RUN under Darling; C & C++ compile+run. The blockers were all environment, fixed in the test prefix (darling-prefix-homebrew-test):

1. SDK DETECTION — CLT MacOSX.sdk ships only SDKSettings.plist (10.13); Homebrew read_sdk_version only parses SDKSettings.json, so MacOS.sdk=nil → 'does not support macOS 11' gate. FIX: wrote SDKSettings.json (Version 11.3) into the active SDK. Permanent: ship SDKSettings.json in Darling's own SDK (Developer/Platforms/MacOSX.platform/Developer/SDKs/MacOSX.sdk, git-tracked).

2. LINKER -oso_prefix — Homebrew's cc shim passes -oso_prefix on every link; ld64-305 (Xcode 9 CLT) errors 'unknown option' (added in Xcode 11 / ld64-507). FIX (3 edits, upstream-style, mirrors no_fixup_chains/ld_classic gates): shared.rb oso_prefix_support? = ld64_version>=507; os/mac super.rb determine_cccfg appends 'o'; cc shim oso_prefix? requires config.include?('o'). Under Darling ld64_version is blank → flag dropped.

3. SANDBOX — build runs under sandbox-exec -f homebrew.sb; disabled via HOMEBREW_NO_SANDBOX=1 (made mac sandbox.rb available? honor it). Belt-and-suspenders; macOS sandbox is meaningless under emulation.

4. *** FINAL ROOT BLOCKER = Darling VFS bug (see new issue) *** — make install populates the keg correctly (a fresh find/ls process AND the host see bin/lz4, lib/liblz4.dylib, headers, etc.), but the long-lived parent brew (ruby) process that created the keg dir empty before the build sees it STILL EMPTY when it re-globs → raises 'Empty installation' and rm_r rolls it back. Cross-process directory-entry coherence: a process that cached a dir doesn't see entries written into it by another process. Worked around with HOMEBREW_FRESH_KEG_CHECK=1 (re-check keg via fresh subprocess find) → full green install. The real fix belongs in Darling's VFS.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
TOOLCHAIN ARCHITECTURE + oso_prefix RE-ANALYSIS (2026-06-11) — corrects an earlier wrong attribution.

DARLING SHIPS NO COMPILER. /usr/bin/{clang,clang++,cc,gcc,ld,as} in the system layer are all the SAME 12660-byte Mach-O: xcrun trampolines (src/xcselect/xcrun-shim.c -> xcselect_invoke_xcrun). They compile/link nothing; they locate the real toolchain via xcode-select/DEVELOPER_DIR and re-exec it inside an installed CLT or Xcode. README confirms the design: 'install Xcode along with its toolchain and SDKs' and invoke its clang directly. So 'use Darling's own tools instead of CLT' is not possible for compilation — there is no Darling clang. CLT/Xcode is REQUIRED for clang + SDK headers/frameworks.

What Darling DOES build itself: cctools-port binutils only (ld64, as, otool, nm) at src/external/cctools-port. Notably its ld64 DOES support -oso_prefix (ld64/src/ld/Options.cpp:4078). BUT /usr/bin/ld is the xcrun trampoline -> CLT's ld, NOT the cctools ld. So in practice the CLT linker is what runs.

WHY -oso_prefix FAILS: the container's CLT ld is Apple prebuilt ld64-305 (Xcode 9; verified via strings: PROJECT:ld64-305). -oso_prefix was added in ld64-507 (Xcode 11). Homebrew's cc shim emits -oso_prefix unconditionally, ld64-305 errors 'unknown option'.

CORRECTED ATTRIBUTION: this is NEITHER a Darling source bug (Darling's own ld supports the flag) NOR clearly a Homebrew bug (emitting -oso_prefix is correct for toolchains Homebrew supports; Xcode 9/ld64-305 is BELOW Homebrew's support floor, so upstream would likely reject a gating patch, not accept it). It is an ENVIRONMENT mismatch: running against an unsupported, too-old Apple CLT. My earlier note calling the 3-edit Homebrew oso_prefix gate an 'upstream Homebrew' concern was an unjustified pattern-match (the workaround lives in Homebrew code and mirrors Homebrew's own ld64-version gates, but that does not make upstream the right home). The Homebrew 3-edit gate is a LOCAL workaround, not an upstream candidate.

TWO REAL FIXES (instead of the Homebrew patch):
  (A) Route linking through Darling's own cctools-port ld64 (oso_prefix-capable) while keeping CLT clang — e.g. clang -fuse-ld=<darling cctools ld>, or install cctools ld ahead of the trampoline. Needs verifying CLT clang-900 drives the newer ld cleanly.
  (B) Install a newer CLT (ld64>=507). See new bead for the CLT-upgrade path.

title: PR draft: mldr — reset glibc loader locks in raw-fork child
description: Upstream the mldr glibc raw-fork child reset hardening committed locally as 5fb98a598 on fix/homebrew-psynch-ruby-hang. Darling uses raw __NR_fork, bypassing glibc's normal fork() child-side loader reset, so mldr now detects and resets the relevant glibc rtld locks in __mldr_postfork_child(): the existing stack-cache lock plus dl_load_lock and dl_load_tls_lock. The reset logic lives in src/startup/mldr/glibc_fork_reset.{c,h}, is wired into mldr.c/CMakeLists, and has a deterministic regression test under tests/regression/run-glibc-fork-lock-reset.sh. Validation: regression PASS; ninja mldr mldr32 PASS; ninja src/startup/mldr/install PASS; lz4 --build-from-source e2e smoke 3/3 ALL-PASS against rebuilt/installed mldr. Relates to dar-q95.12 (xnu side: call __mldr_postfork_child in fork child).
notes: Patch-vs-root audit: this is not a random lock workaround. It restores the child-side host-runtime cleanup skipped by Darling's raw __NR_fork path. The broader rule for future review is that any critical host libc/runtime child reset skipped by raw fork needs an explicit mldr equivalent.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
=== dar-gwn.5 HARDENING via TDD: reset ALL glibc loader locks in the raw-fork child (not just stack-cache) ===

CONTEXT / WHY
The shipped fix (4a0a80cf7) resets only GL(_dl_stack_cache_lock) in the fork child.
Reviewing against glibc 2.39 source shows that is an INCOMPLETE copy of what glibc's
own fork() does. glibc's __libc_fork child path (posix/fork.c:100-109) resets, UNCONDITIONALLY:
  __rtld_lock_initialize(GL(dl_load_lock));       // dynamic-loader lock (dlopen/dlclose)
  __rtld_lock_initialize(GL(dl_load_tls_lock));   // dynamic-TLS lock (TLS setup)
  reclaim_stacks();                               // resets _dl_stack_cache_lock + lists
By contrast _Fork() (sysdeps/nptl/_Fork.c) resets ONLY the robust-mutex list. Darling
forks with raw __NR_fork (sys_fork in .../bsd/impl/process/fork.c:27) = _Fork-level, so it
inherits ALL of these loader locks in whatever state the parent left them. The stack-cache
lock is just the one we happened to hit; _dl_load_lock (held during any dlopen) and
_dl_load_tls_lock (held during TLS setup / dlopen of TLS-bearing objects) are the SAME class
of latent deadlock. brew/ruby/perl dlopen at runtime, so these are reachable.

GLIBC FACTS (verified, glibc-2.39 source; cloned to /tmp/glibc-ref):
- struct rtld_global field order (sysdeps/generic/ldsodefs.h):
    _dl_load_lock (366), _dl_load_write_lock (370), _dl_load_tls_lock (377)  <- THREE consecutive
      __rtld_lock_recursive_t, NO fields between them (only comments).
    ... (large arch-dependent gap: _dl_ns[16], link_map, x86 cpu_features, TLS slotinfo) ...
    _dl_stack_used (474) / _dl_stack_user (477) / _dl_stack_cache (480) /
    _dl_stack_cache_actsize (483) / _dl_in_flight_stack (488) / _dl_stack_cache_lock (491).
- __rtld_lock_recursive_t = struct { pthread_mutex_t mutex; }  (sysdeps/nptl/libc-lockP.h:39)
- free/initialized value = _RTLD_LOCK_RECURSIVE_INITIALIZER = {PTHREAD_RECURSIVE_MUTEX_INITIALIZER_NP}
  => pthread_mutex_t with every field 0 EXCEPT __kind == PTHREAD_MUTEX_RECURSIVE_NP (==1).
- runtime takes them via __pthread_mutex_lock (recursive), NOT the rtld inline path
  (libc-lockP.h:130, the !IS_IN(rtld) branch).

DETECTION STRATEGY (robust, version-independent, self-validating)
The three loader locks are three back-to-back free recursive mutexes (40 bytes each on LP64).
Nothing else in rtld_global places three recursive mutexes at a 40-byte stride (the per-namespace
unique_sym_table.lock mutexes are separated by the whole link_namespaces struct), so the FIRST
such triple uniquely identifies _dl_load_lock / _dl_load_write_lock / _dl_load_tls_lock.
Anchor the scan to end at the (already reliably found) stack-cache region.
GROUND-TRUTH CONFIRMED: verify.c parks a thread inside a dlopen ELF constructor (holds the real
_dl_load_lock); the detected address then reads __lock=1 __owner=<holder tid> __count=1 -> the
detected pointer IS the real lock. (Also note: process exit() then deadlocks on that held lock,
which is itself a confirmation.)

A SUBTLE FACT THE TDD SURFACED (important, affects how the bug actually triggers):
A raw __NR_fork does NOT refresh glibc's cached THREAD_SELF->tid in the child. So in the child the
forking thread still believes its tid == the PARENT's tid. Consequence for RECURSIVE mutexes:
the deadlock only manifests when the held lock's __owner is a DIFFERENT thread than the forker
(the normal case: thread B in dlopen while thread A forks). If __owner happened to equal the
forker's tid, the recursive mutex would just re-enter. The stack-cache lock is a plain LLL int
(no owner check) so it always deadlocks regardless. The test simulates a foreign owner accordingly.

THE FIX (validated at unit level; not yet ported into the repo)
Extend the child reset to also reset the two recursive loader locks to
_RTLD_LOCK_RECURSIVE_INITIALIZER (memset 0, __kind=RECURSIVE, release __lock last):
    if (g_load_lock)     reset_recursive_mutex(g_load_lock);
    if (g_load_tls_lock) reset_recursive_mutex(g_load_tls_lock);
plus the existing stack-cache reset. Code: glibc_fork_reset.{c,h} in this dir.
NOTE ON SCOPE: I deliberately did NOT reimplement the full reclaim_stacks() list hygiene
(walking _dl_stack_used to recycle dead-thread stacks, __nptl_nthreads=1, fork-generation bump).
That part needs struct-pthread offsets (very version-fragile) and is NOT a deadlock source — it
is bookkeeping/optimization. Resetting the three LOCKS is the complete deadlock fix; the existing
"empty the stack cache list" keeps that subsystem self-consistent.

TDD RESULTS (host glibc 2.39, harness in this dir; build/run below)
RED  (reset disabled): all 3 cases deadlock (stack_cache/pthread, load_lock/dlopen, load_tls_lock/dlopen).
RED  (old reset = stack-cache only): stack_cache PASSES, load_lock + load_tls_lock STILL deadlock.
GREEN(new reset, +loader locks): all 3 PASS, and all 3 still deadlock when reset is disabled (test stays meaningful).

HARNESS (this directory)
- glibc_fork_reset.{c,h} : the module (detect + child reset). Drop-in for mldr (replaces the inline
  code in src/startup/mldr/mldr.c lines ~593-647; mldr's __mldr_postfork_child should call
  __mldr_glibc_fork_reset_child() then reset its own socket_bitmap.mutex; main() calls
  __mldr_glibc_fork_reset_detect() early, where it currently calls __mldr_detect_stack_cache_lock()).
- test.c       : deterministic RED/GREEN deadlock harness (3 cases x reset on/off). Exit 0 = all green.
- tls_mod.c    : a TLS-bearing .so so dlopen exercises _dl_load_tls_lock.
- park_mod.c   : ELF-constructor parks forever (holds real _dl_load_lock) for ground truth.
- verify.c     : confirms the detected load_lock address tracks the real lock.
Build & run:
    gcc -shared -fPIC -O0 -g tls_mod.c  -o tls_mod.so
    gcc -shared -fPIC -O0 -g park_mod.c -o park_mod.so
    gcc -O0 -g -DGLIBC_FORK_RESET_TEST_HOOKS test.c   glibc_fork_reset.c -o test   -ldl -lpthread
    gcc -O0 -g -DGLIBC_FORK_RESET_TEST_HOOKS verify.c glibc_fork_reset.c -o verify -ldl -lpthread
    ./test    # expect "RESULT: ALL GREEN", exit 0
    timeout 10 ./verify

REMAINING WORK (handoff)

title: PR: darlingserver — cancel stale wait_timer in thread_unblock
description: Upstream darlingserver commit 32af4d56: thread_unblock should cancel an armed wait_timer when a timed wait is woken early, matching XNU and preventing stale timer expiry from injecting a spurious THREAD_TIMED_OUT into a later unrelated wait. Needs clean branch off darlingserver origin/main with [PH]/KWQDBG/CALLDBG diagnostics excluded.

title: PR: libpthread — decode negative psynch wait returns
description: Upstream libpthread commit 3de6464: Darling's emulated psynch waits can return BSD errors as negative return-register values rather than macOS-style -1 + errno. Decode negative returns in condvar/mutex/rwlock waits so EINTR/ETIMEDOUT/prepost recovery paths run. Needs clean branch off darling-libpthread upstream; consider whether normalizing libsystem_kernel syscall ABI is the deeper alternative.
notes: Patch-vs-root audit: this is the most suspicious layering item. The local libpthread decoder works as a compatibility backstop for negative BSD errno returns, but it may encode a Darling-specific kernel ABI convention into libpthread. Resolve dar-q95.23 before opening this PR; if libsystem_kernel can normalize to Darwin/macOS-style -1 + errno, reduce or drop this libpthread divergence.

title: PR: xnu — implement getattrlist ATTR_CMN_NAME and ATTR_CMN_OBJTYPE
description: Upstream xnu/libsystem_kernel commit d157c3f: implement getattrlist ATTR_CMN_NAME + ATTR_CMN_OBJTYPE so Darwin Ruby Dir.glob and Homebrew empty_installation? work. Top-level pointer bump is 7df268d2d.

title: PR: xnu — implement getattrlistbulk
description: Upstream xnu/libsystem_kernel commit 6159147: implement getattrlistbulk instead of ENOTSUP for directory enumeration APIs. Top-level pointer bump is 4b504ae86.

title: PR: xnu — return psynch wait errors as negative errno
description: Upstream xnu/libsystem_kernel commits f3c8d94/0a10e19: psynch wait wrappers return BSD wait errors as negative errno values in the return register for Darling's emulated path. Pair with libpthread negative-return decode or reconsider normalizing to macOS -1+errno ABI.
notes: Patch-vs-root audit: paired with dar-q95.16 and blocked on dar-q95.23. Decide whether negative BSD errno is the intended emulated psynch ABI, or whether this layer should normalize wait failures to macOS-style -1 + errno so libpthread stays closer to upstream.

title: PR: xnu — psynch cvsignal/cvbroad argument fixes
description: cvlsgen/mugen uint32->uint64 in sys_psynch_cvsignal; psynch_cvbroad pass mutex (not mugen). Part of commit 3b236c0.

title: PR: xnu — honor SA_RESTART for sigexc handlers
description: Upstream xnu/libsystem_kernel commit b8d9ccc plus top-level 7f61970a7: mirror Darwin SA_RESTART behavior for signal exception handlers; includes ruby_thread_kill_read regression.

title: PR: top-level darling — ship sandbox-exec pass-through
description: Upstream top-level commit f85f220f0: install a sandbox-exec(1) pass-through command so Homebrew's sandbox path can run without HOMEBREW_NO_SANDBOX. Review whether pass-through behavior is acceptable or should advertise unsupported policy semantics.
notes: Patch-vs-root audit: compatibility shim only. Do not present as sandbox semantics or a sandbox implementation; it only satisfies build tools that expect sandbox-exec to exist. Upstream review should decide pass-through vs warning vs explicit unsupported-policy failure.

title: PR: top-level darling — add SDKSettings and versioned SDK symlink
description: Upstream top-level commits 75437f43b and 3c79b03fa: add SDKSettings.json and rename MacOSX10.13.sdk symlink to MacOSX11.sdk so Homebrew detects the bundled SDK and passes check_broken_sdks.

title: Resolve psynch wait error ABI layering before upstreaming negative-return fixes
description:
Audit whether Darling should expose emulated psynch wait failures to libpthread as negative BSD errno return-register values, or normalize the libsystem_kernel ABI to macOS-style -1 plus errno before libpthread sees them.

Context from patch-vs-root review:
- dar-q95.16 currently teaches libpthread to decode negative psynch wait returns. This is the most suspicious layer because it bakes a Darling-specific kernel ABI shape into libpthread.
- dar-q95.19 currently documents and returns negative errno from the emulated psynch wait wrappers.
- A more root-correct fix may normalize the emulated libsystem_kernel/syscall boundary so libpthread remains closer to upstream Darwin behavior.

Acceptance criteria:
- Decide and document the intended ABI at the emulated libsystem_kernel/libpthread boundary for psynch waits.
- If macOS-style -1 + errno is feasible, update/split dar-q95.16 and dar-q95.19 drafts accordingly and reduce libpthread divergence.
- If negative errno is intentionally kept, document why it is required and keep libpthread decoding as an explicit compatibility backstop.
- Re-run the psynch/Homebrew regression path affected by condvar, mutex, and rwlock interrupted/timed waits.

title: PR: darlingserver — fix microthread resume-before-suspend lost wakeup (coalesced resume permit)
description: SUPERSEDES the original reply-deferral framing (that diagnosis was ruled out, see dar-gwn.1). The real darlingserver fix is the generic suspend/resume lost-wakeup race (dar-gwn.1.4): a wake arriving before/during physical suspension was dropped. Fix = coalesced _resumePermit covering early/in-progress/late wakeups (Thread::resume/suspend/doWork). Commits b6a4ce3 (Fix microthread resume-before-suspend race) + 26f50b5 (Document resume permit protocol). The dedicated per-timer-expiry microthread workaround (dar-gwn.1.3, commit 8ff03f0) was REMOVED/net-reverted because timer delivery on shared kernelAsync passes once this race is fixed. Validated: deterministic 20ms amplified RED 3/3 timeout -> GREEN 10/10; ordinary 50/50; heavy 80-thread/40k-wait stable RSS ~27 MiB. Black-box + white-box reproducers under tools/repro-darlingserver-resume-race/. Local branch fix/microthread-resume-race off darlingserver origin/main (excludes [PH] debug tracing in duct-tape/src/thread.c).
notes: Original blocker on dar-gwn.1.3 (timer fix) is obsolete: timer workaround removed by this fix.
